title: Simple Play Kitchen Narrow Fridge
summary: A narrow toy refrigerator with a freezer-over-fridge door layout, built from 1x12s. Pairs with a play-kitchen set; a durable, years-of-use gift.
description:
This is the fridge that completes a play kitchen — a tall narrow box with two doors, a small freezer up top over a taller fridge below, sized to sit beside a play stove and sink. It's just a plywood-free box of 1x12 boards, which keeps it solid and paintable, and it holds up to years of small-child abuse.

The build is honest and quick: nail and glue the box (the source builder used a pneumatic nailer and glue and reports it's still going strong years later), add the back nailing into the internal shelves for rigidity, then hang the two doors. The doors are the only place to fuss — euro-style inset hinges are concealed, cheap, and easy to mount, and they let the doors sit flush and swing cleanly. Magnetic clasps keep them shut so the play food stays put.

Because it's a toy, round over the door edges and corners, and use a durable, child-safe finish. The door boards may need trimming to fit your actual openings — measure before final cut.

tools.note: Square crosscuts on the 1x12s and back panel.
The whole box is glued and pin-nailed.
For mounting the hinges, clasps, and handles.
Round every edge — it's a toy.

materials.name: Child-safe finish
materials.note: Durable and rated safe once cured.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 12 ft 1x12, cut two sides at 36", three tops/bottoms at 11-1/2", the freezer door at 12" and the fridge door at 20-1/4"; that is 138-3/4" of parts plus kerfs, so the single declared board covers it with a few inches spare. Cut the fridge shelf at 11-1/2" from the 1x10 and the two bottom trim pieces at 11-1/2" from the 1x2. The back is the one part to check before you buy: the cut list calls for 13" x 36", and 13" is the number the geometry requires (11-1/2" between two 3/4" sides), so the materials line's 12" x 36" panel is an inch too narrow. Square every cut, and label the three 11-1/2" tops/bottoms and the 11-1/2" shelf as you go — they are the same length but the shelf is 2" shallower.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: The two 36" sides run the full height of the box and every horizontal member fits between them, which is why the tops/bottoms are 11-1/2" and the finished case is 13" wide, 36" tall and 11-1/4" deep. Stand the sides on edge and glue and pin the bottom, the middle divider that separates freezer from fridge, and the top between them, keeping all front edges flush; the three 3/4" members leave 33-3/4" of clear opening, and the doors at 12" and 20-1/4" account for 32-1/4" of it, so set the divider to give a 12" freezer opening above a 20-1/4" fridge opening and let the bottom trim close the last 1-1/2". Add the 9-1/4"-deep fridge shelf inside the lower compartment, glued and pinned through the sides, remembering it stops 2" short of the back of the case. Nail the two 11-1/2" bottom trim pieces across the base, glue every joint, and check the case for square by measuring the diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the back
steps.body: Lay the case face down, check the diagonals one more time, and set the 13" x 36" plywood back onto it — the back is what holds the case square, so square it first and nail second. Glue the edges and run 1-1/4" brads into both 36" sides, the top, the bottom and the middle divider, roughly every 4" to 6". Do not aim for the fridge shelf: it is a 9-1/4"-deep 1x10 in an 11-1/4"-deep case, so its back edge is 2" in front of the panel and any nail driven at it comes out into open air.

steps.title: Hang the doors and add hardware
steps.body: Measure your actual openings and trim the 12" freezer door and the 20-1/4" fridge door to leave a small even gap around each before you drill for hinges — the doors are single 11-1/4"-wide boards in a 13" case, so a little trimming is expected rather than a sign something went wrong. Bore the hinge cups and mount the euro-style inset concealed hinges, then adjust the cams until both doors sit flush and their gaps match. Add a magnetic clasp at the free edge of each door and a handle on each, positioning the freezer handle low and the fridge handle high so a small hand reaches both. Round over every edge and corner with the sander — this is a toy that will be climbed on as well as played with — then apply the child-safe finish and let it cure fully before it goes in the playroom.

images.alt: A narrow wooden toy refrigerator with a freezer-over-fridge door layout for a play kitchen
